description:
You are a QA engineer preparing a local test environment and need to perform a very quick connectivity check that can later be consumed by automated tooling.

Your goal:
1. Create a new directory  
   /home/user/network_test  
   (the directory will not exist beforehand).

2. Inside that directory, create a single plain-text log file named  
   ping_result.log

3. The log must contain exactly the raw console output produced by issuing ONE single-line IPv4 ICMP echo request to the local host, i.e. a one-packet ping to 127.0.0.1.  
   • Use the canonical `ping` utility already present on all Linux systems.  
   • The command must send precisely one packet (count = 1).  
   • Do not add any extra redirects, banners, comment lines, or blank lines—store only what `ping` prints to STDOUT/STDERR.

Required output format that the automated grader will check:
• The file must exist at /home/user/network_test/ping_result.log.  
• Its content must include a summary line containing the exact phrase  
  “1 received”  
  (the grader will ignore surrounding text but the phrase must appear verbatim, case-sensitive).  
• No additional files should be placed in /home/user/network_test.

Once finished, there is nothing else to configure—only be sure that the directory and file exist with the correct content.
truth:
Expected artefacts for verification:
1. Directory: /home/user/network_test (permissions 755 or more relaxed).
2. File: /home/user/network_test/ping_result.log
   - The grader will:
     a) Assert that the file exists.
     b) Read the file and search for the regex /\b1 received\b/.
        If the phrase is present (exact case), the task passes.
The rest of the file content (timestamps, latency values, etc.) is not evaluated.
